version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch aims to log device I/O errors for all bio errors, not just BLK_STS_IOERR and BLK_STS_TARGET. This is done by removing the conditional checks in btrfs_log_dev_io_error() and instead asserting that the bio status is valid.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewed the patch and found it to be a good change. The current implementation of error logging is indeed limited and this patch fixes that.
review_comments.raw_body:
\u5728 2026/3/14 05:56, Boris Burkov \u5199\u9053:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>

Thanks,
Qu


> ---
>   fs/btrfs/bio.c | 3 +--
>   1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/bio.c b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> index 2a2a21aec817..08b1d62603d0 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> @@ -352,8 +352,7 @@ static void btrfs_log_dev_io_error(const struct bio *bio, struct btrfs_device *d
>   {
>   	if (!dev || !dev->bdev)
>   		return;
> -	if (bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_IOERR && bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_TARGET)
> -		return;
> +	ASSERT(bio->bi_status);
>   
>   	if (btrfs_op(bio) == BTRFS_MAP_WRITE)
>   		btrfs_dev_stat_inc_and_print(dev, BTRFS_DEV_STAT_WRITE_ERRS);

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Suggested leaving the current implementation as-is for now, but promised to discuss and address the block status vs file system issues soon.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 12:26:05PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().

This goes all the way back to adding the error counts in commit
442a4f6308e69.  The block error values are a bit of a mess and it's
on my todo list to sort them out - only a small number of them should
even be seen here.  I'd suggest to leave things as-is for now, and
I'll make sure you're included in the discussion when I get around
to attack the block status vs file system issues, which will hopefully
be very soon.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Responded to Christoph's suggestion by pointing out that logging the errors they are already seeing would be beneficial. Offered to help ensure btrfs does what Christoph wants when he addresses the block status issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Mar 16, 2026 at 05:20:20PM +0100, Christoph Hellwig wrote:
> On Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 12:26:05PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> > As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> > these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> > bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> > layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> > system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> > 
> > For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> > BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> > BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> This goes all the way back to adding the error counts in commit
> 442a4f6308e69.  The block error values are a bit of a mess and it's
> on my todo list to sort them out - only a small number of them should
> even be seen here.  I'd suggest to leave things as-is for now, and
> I'll make sure you're included in the discussion when I get around
> to attack the block status vs file system issues, which will hopefully
> be very soon.
> 

Is there a reason not to properly log the errors we are definitely
already seeing? I have thousands of instances with swallowed
BLK_STS_MEDIUM errors that really confused me and messed up my data
while investigating a possible regression.

If you think this will confuse/upset users who aren't watching a giant
fleet with distributed logging and make them throw out good drives
prematurely or something, then I can just carry this patch internally.

If you are just worried about having to aim at a moving target, I am
happy to offer to be available to make sure btrfs does what you want
when you do find time to work on this. It's hard for me to judge since I
don't really know what is wrong with the error signalling as-is.

Put another way, if we are in a situation with technical debt, which is
what I think you are saying, then I would rather have more visibility
rather than less.

Thanks,
Boris

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/2] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides additional information such as hold time estimation and the holder's stack at release time, which can be useful for debugging and performance analysis. The new tracepoint has minimal impact on performance and binary size. It was previously discussed in an RFC (Request For Comments) and this patch addresses some of the feedback received.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif pointed out that the patch does not consistently check for blocked waiters, specifically suggesting to use waitqueue_active(&sem->waiters) in percpu_up_write() to match other calls.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello!

I saw that you mentioned in the commmit message that you do this for only
blocked waiters except for percpu_up_write(). We can use
waitqueue_active(&sem->waiters) to check for this over here so that
its consistent with every other call?

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledged a potential issue with using waitqueue_active() in the tracepoint instrumentation, agreeing that wq_has_sleeper() is more suitable since it doesn't require holding the lock and adding a barrier.

Author acknowledged a concern about the timing of the contended_release tracepoint and moved it to fire after the lock is released but before rcuwait_wake_up(), agreeing to restructure in v3
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the feedback, Usama.

I thought about it and even mentioned in the comment, but I forgot what
was the reason. Now, I think you are correct. I added wq_has_sleeper()
locally instead of waitqueue_active() locally, since we are not holding
the lock here and waitqueue_active() requires a barrier based on the
comment. It might be not very important here, but I'd rather make it
correct even for tracepoint.

Note that __percpu_up_read() doesn't need this guard. Maybe I was
thinking at __percpu_up_read() part before and just made it symmetric.

Anyway, thanks for suggestion.

---

Good point. I moved it after this_cpu_dec() so the tracepoint fires
after the lock is released but before rcuwait_wake_up(). I also went
through all other call sites and made the placement consistent where
possible: after release, before wake. It should be fixed in v3.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release, which fires when a lock is released under contention. The patch complements existing contention_begin and contention_end tracepoints by providing information from the holder's perspective. It enables correlating lock hold time under contention with waiter events by lock address. The patch introduces calls to trace_contended_release() in slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks, including mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/2] EVM: add comment describing why ino field is still unsigned long
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a comment to the EVM code explaining why the inode number field is still an unsigned long, despite the inode->i_ino being widened to u64. series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 02/17] support/export: remove unnecessary static variables in nfsd_fh expkey lookup
series_items.patch_summary: The patch removes unnecessary static variables in the nfsd_fh expkey lookup function, converting them to local variables scoped to the outer for loop.

series_items.patch_summary: This patch series removes outdated documentation from the exportfs manpage, specifically legacy mode information for 2.4 and earlier kernels that used the nfsservctl() syscall.

series_items.patch_summary: This patch series updates the netlink headers for all cache upcalls in the nfs-utils package, syncing them with the current kernel UAPI header and adding a new bundled sunrpc_netlink.h header for the sunrpc genl family.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 05/17] build: add libnl3 and netlink header support for exportd and mountd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for libnl3 and netlink headers to the exportd and mountd builds in nfs-utils. It updates the configure.ac file to include checks for libnl3, sunrpc_netlink.h, and linux/nfsd_netlink.h. The changes ensure that the system header includes expkey support by checking for NFSD_A_EXPKEY_SEQNO.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 06/17] xlog: claim D_FAC3 as D_NETLINK

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 07/17] exportd/mountd: add netlink support for svc_export cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based support for the svc_export cache, allowing userspace to receive notifications of pending export requests from the kernel and respond accordingly. The existing procfs channel mechanism is retained as a fallback in case the kernel does not support the nfsd genetlink family or the 'exportd' multicast group.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the potential for duplicate processing of export requests and suggested adding a check to prevent it. Also asked for more information on how the netlink setup silently fails in older kernels.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 08/17] exportd/mountd: add netlink support for the nfsd.fh cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series refactors the exportd/mountd code to add netlink support for the nfsd.fh cache, allowing for more efficient and scalable handling of expkey requests.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 09/17] exportd/mountd: add netlink support for the auth.unix.ip cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series refactors the netlink support for the auth.unix.ip cache in the NFS-utils codebase, introducing a generic helper function and converting existing code to use it. The sunrpc genl family socket setup is also added for handling upcalls from the kernel.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified potential issues with the patch series, including missing error handling and unclear code paths. Requested clarification on the purpose of the generic helper function and suggested improvements to the existing code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 10/17] exportd/mountd: add netlink support for the auth.unix.gid cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds netlink support for the auth.unix.gid cache in the NFS-utils exportd and mountd components. The goal is to improve performance by reducing the number of getpwuid() and getgrouplist() calls. The patch introduces a new handler that resolves UIDs to group lists using netlink messages.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 11/17] mountd/exportd: only use /proc interfaces if netlink setup fails
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series modifies the mountd and exportd components of nfs-utils to use netlink interfaces instead of /proc channel files, except in cases where netlink setup fails.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 12/17] support/export: check for pending requests after opening netlink sockets
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces support for checking pending requests after opening netlink sockets in the NFS-utils code. The goal is to ensure that any queued requests are processed before mountd comes online.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 13/17] exportd/mountd: use cache type from notifications to target scanning
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the exportd/mountd components of the nfs-utils package to use cache type information from netlink multicast notifications to target scanning. It parses NFSD_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_CACHE_TYPE and SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_CACHE_TYPE attributes, determines which caches have pending requests, and only issues GET_REQS for those caches, reducing unnecessary netlink round-trips.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 14/17] nfsd/sunrpc: add cache flush command and attribute enums
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for flushing nfsd and sunrpc caches via generic netlink commands, allowing userspace to selectively flush specific caches instead of writing to /proc/net/rpc/* files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 15/17] exportfs: add netlink support for cache flush with /proc fallback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink support for cache flush operations in the exportfs module, providing a fallback to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush if the sunrpc genl family cannot be resolved on older kernels.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 16/17] exportfs: use netlink to probe kernel support, skip export_test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the exportfs module to use netlink to probe kernel support for new cache interfaces, allowing it to skip an advisory export test.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a --no-netlink option to disable netlink support in the nfs-utils package, allowing users to fall back to /proc interfaces instead. This is useful for debugging, testing, or working around kernel netlink issues.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 14/14] nfsd: add NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH netlink command

series_items.subject: [PATCH 00/14] nfsd/sunrpc: add support for netlink upcalls for mountd/exportd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for netlink upcalls to the NFS server and client, replacing antiquated interfaces in /proc. The new mechanism uses a generic netlink family and allows for more flexible and efficient handling of cache updates.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: There is no review comment from Jeff Layton in the provided thread.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 01/14] nfsd: move struct nfsd_genl_rqstp to nfsctl.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the definition of struct nfsd_genl_rqstp from fs/nfsd/nfsd.h to fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c, where it is used. This change aims to keep related definitions together in a single file.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 02/14] sunrpc: rename sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() to sunrpc_cache_upcall()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() to sunrpc_cache_upcall() in preparation for its future use of netlink for upcalls, allowing it to be more flexible and adaptable.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the rename, suggesting that it might be better to introduce a new function for netlink upcalls instead of modifying an existing one.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/14] sunrpc: rename sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout() to sunrpc_cache_upcall_warn(), as it no longer represents a timeout but instead warns if there are no listeners.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: The function name was misleading and did not accurately reflect its purpose. The rename to sunrpc_cache_upcall_warn() is a good correction.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 04/14] sunrpc: rename cache_pipe_upcall() to cache_do_upcall()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the cache_pipe_upcall() function to cache_do_upcall() in preparation for handling netlink upcalls, which will also be handled by this function.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: The patch is a simple rename of the function, which will be used for both pipe and netlink upcalls.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 05/14] sunrpc: add a cache_notify callback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new cache_notify operation to struct cache_detail, which will be used by a later patch to send netlink notifications when there is a pending cache_request.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/14] sunrpc: add helpers to count and snapshot pending cache requests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds helpers to count and snapshot pending cache requests in the sunrpc subsystem, allowing callers to safely access the request list without exposing internal struct cache_request details.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the use of cache_get() reference in sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(), suggesting an alternative approach to ensure safe access to the request list.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/14] sunrpc: add a generic netlink family for cache upcalls
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a generic netlink family for cache upcalls in the sunrpc module, addressing the limitation of existing caches living within the module. It defines a YAML spec and implements the necessary code to support cache notifications.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the implementation of sunrpc_cache_notify() and suggested using a more generic approach to handle cache notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/14] sunrpc: add netlink upcall for the auth.unix.ip cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the ip_map (auth.unix.ip) cache, including new attributes and operations in the sunrpc_cache YAML spec, and updates to the net/sunrpc/netlink.c and net/sunrpc/svcauth_unix.c files.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: No comments from David Hildenbrand in the thread so far.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/14] sunrpc: add netlink upcall for the auth.unix.gid cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the unix_gid (auth.unix.gid) cache in the sunrpc subsystem. It introduces a new attribute-set and operations to manage pending requests and responses, and wires up a callback to trigger multicast events on cache misses.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the implementation of sunrpc_nl_unix_gid_get_reqs_dumpit() and suggested a different approach to handling pending requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/14] nfsd: add new netlink spec for svc_export upcall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new netlink specification for NFSd to handle svc_export upcalls, including definitions and attribute sets for cache notify, RPC status, file system location, authentication flavor, and service export requests.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the patch's complexity and potential impact on performance, suggesting a more incremental approach to implementing netlink support for NFSd.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 11/14] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the svc_export cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the svc_export (nfsd.export) cache, enabling userspace to receive notifications about pending requests and updates.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the implementation of nfsd_cache_notify() and suggested alternative approaches to handle cache notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 12/14] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the nfsd.fh cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the nfsd.fh cache, including a new expkey attribute-set and operations to handle client requests and updates.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: The patch series implements netlink-based cache upcall support for the nfsd.fh cache, including a new expkey attribute-set and operations to handle client requests and updates. The code adds a new enum value for expkey, a new attribute-set with fields for client, fsidtype, fsid, negative flag, expiry, and path, and two new operations to dump pending expkey requests and parse responses from userspace.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 13/14] sunrpc: add SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH netlink command
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH generic netlink command to flush sunrpc auth caches without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command allows userspace to select which caches to flush using an optional attribute.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH generic netlink command to allow userspace to flush nfsd export caches without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command takes an optional u32 attribute to select which caches to flush, and falls back to the /proc-based cache_flush() for older kernels.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: No substantive comments from David Hildenbrand in the thread.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v5 1/4] openat2: new OPENAT2_REGULAR flag support
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag called OPENAT2_REGULAR to the openat2() function, which restricts file opens to regular files only and returns an error for non-regular files. The patch also includes filesystem-specific checks in ceph, gfs2, nfs, and cifs to ensure that resources are properly cleaned up when returning errors. However, two issues were found: the flag leaks into f_flags and a BUILD_BUG_ON check was not updated. These issues need to be fixed before the patch can be applied.

review_comments.author: Dorjoy Chowdhury
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Dorjoy Chowdhury agreed that the issues found by Claude were valid and suggested specific modifications to fix them: adding OPENAT2_REGULAR to the flags cleared in do_dentry_open, and updating VALID_OPEN_FLAGS to VALID_OPENAT2_FLAGS in fs/open.c and fs/fcntl.c
review_comments.raw_body:
Good catches! I guess for issue 1 I need to modify the line in
do_dentry_open implementation to
f->f_flags &= ~(O_CREAT | O_EXCL | O_NOCTTY | O_TRUNC | OPENAT2_REGULAR);
right?
And for issue 2, I should change the VALID_OPEN_FLAGS to
VALID_OPENAT2_FLAGS in both build_open_flags in fs/open.c and in
fcntl_init in fs/fcntl.c, correct?

Regards,
Dorjoy

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCHBLIZZARD v7] fuse/libfuse/e2fsprogs: containerize ext4 for safer operation
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch proposes containerizing ext4 for safer operation, allowing for isolation and security benefits without requiring near-native performance. The approach involves using lklfuse, which is a user-space filesystem that uses the Linux kernel's virtual machine (VM) to run a kernel instance in user space. This method has pros such as easier setup and maintenance compared to implementing a fuse-iomap server for every filesystem, but may have worse performance. If near-native performance is not a requirement, using lklfuse seems like a less painful option.

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that lklfuse uses user-mode-linux (UML) based on arch/lkl/Kconfig, and suggested an alternative approach by modifying libguestfs to run the fuse server inside a systemd container instead of directly from the CLI.

reviewer clarified that near-native performance is not a strict requirement for the patch's use cases, and suggested that lklfuse might be a more maintainable option if performance is not critical

reviewer noted that a good non-iomap fuse server for every filesystem would be beneficial, and expressed concern about the robustness of libext2fs compared to the kernel

Reviewer noted that using lklfuse would require running a full IO stack, potentially increasing complexity and overhead.

Reviewer noted that btrfs copy-on-write can be handled with fuse-iomap, but other features such as fsverity, fscrypt, and data checksumming are not currently supported by iomap.

Reviewer Darrick Wong expressed skepticism about the potential benefits of using lklfuse, suggesting that it may be easier to maintain than fuse-iomap but also implying that its performance might not be significantly worse.
review_comments.raw_body:
I was under the impression that lklfuse uses UML.  Given the weird
things in arch/lkl/Kconfig:

config 64BIT
	bool "64bit kernel"
	default y if OUTPUT_FORMAT = "pe-x86-64"
	default $(success,$(srctree)/arch/lkl/scripts/cc-objdump-file-format.sh|grep -q '^elf64-') if OUTPUT_FORMAT != "pe-x86-64"

I was kinda guessing x86_64 was the primary target of the developers?

/me notes that he's now looked into libguestfs per Demi Marie's comments
and some curiosity on the part of ngompa and i>

Whatever it is that libguestfs does to stand up unprivileged fs mounts
also could fit this bill.  It's *really* slow to start because it takes
the booted kernel, creates a largeish initramfs, boots that combo via
libvirt, and then fires up a fuse server to talk to the vm kernel.

I think all you'd have to do is change libguestfs to start the VM and
run the fuse server inside a systemd container instead of directly from
the CLI.

---

Not a hard requirement, just a means to convince people that they can
choose containment without completely collapsing performance.

---

Or even a good non-iomap fuse server for every filesystem.  Admittedly
the weak part of fuse4fs is that libext2fs is not as robust as the
kernel is.

---

Probably a lot, because now you have to run a full IO stack all the way
through lklfuse.

---

btrfs cow can be done with iomap, at least on the directio end.  It's
the other features like fsverity/fscrypt/data checksumming that aren't
currently supported by iomap.

---

"Gee, what if I could simplify most of my own work out of existence?"

--D

review_comments.author: Demi Obenour
review_comments.summary:
reviewer emphasized the importance of near-native performance, citing a loop where kernel devs advise against mounting untrusted filesystems but distros continue to do so due to user needs

Reviewer noted that strong verified boot requirements, such as those used by Android and Chromium OS, necessitate mounting the writable partition in userspace to prevent compromised system state on reboot.
sentiment_signals: considered being able to write corrupted metadata to disk a vulnerability
review_comments.raw_body:
It should be good enough that distros are willing to use it by default.
Otherwise, there is no way to break out of the loop where kernel devs
say to not mount untrusted filesystems, yet the distros keep doing
that because people have stuff they need to get done.

I know from talking to Neal Gompa that libguestfs is too slow for this.

---

Anyone with strong verified boot requirements is going to want this.
The threat model used by Android and Chromium OS is that all on-disk
state is *never* trusted.  The root filesystem is on a dm-verity
protected partition, so mounting it in the kernel is fine.  However,
the writable partition should be mounted in userspace.

I checked with the ChromeOS security team, and they do consider being
able to write corrupted metadata to disk that compromises the system
on the next reboot to be a vulnerability.

This is also a case where performance actually matters, including
for F2FS on zoned UFS which is always copy-on-write for user data.
-- 
Sincerely,
Demi Marie Obenour (she/her/hers)

review_comments.author: Demi Obenour
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that libguestfs is too slow for distros, making it unsuitable for mounting untrusted disk images and CI/filesystem testing

reviewer noted that on ChromeOS and Android, only read-only disk images are trusted, making a full fsck at each boot unacceptable due to slow boot times

Reviewer Demi Obenour questioned the performance impact of using lklfuse, asking for a clearer assessment of its performance implications and whether it would be noticeable in normal use.
review_comments.raw_body:
The feedback I have gotten from ngompa is that libguestfs is just
too slow for distros to use it to mount stuff.

---

It's worth noting that on ChromeOS and Android, the only trusted
disk images are those that are read-only and protected by dm-verity.
*Every* writable image is considered untrusted.

I don't know if doing a full fsck at each boot is considered
acceptable, but I suspect it would slow boot far too much.

Yes, Google ought to be paying for the kernel changes to fix this mess.

---

How much is "a lot"?  Is it "this is only useful for non-interactive
overnight backups", "you will notice this in benchmarks but it's okay
for normal use", or somewhere in between?

Could lklfuse and iomap be combined?

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC] [RFC PATCH 0/4] mm/mempolicy: introduce socket-aware weighted interleave
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Proposal to introduce socket-aware weighted interleave in mm/mempolicy, considering a 2x2 situation for cpuset and multi-socket-cpu

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Proposed a simplified approach where cpuset nodes dictate weights, which should already be the case with existing code. Suggested clarifying fallback semantics for allocation
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Mar 16, 2026 at 08:19:32AM -0700, Joshua Hahn wrote:
> 
> In that sense I thought the word "prefer" was a bit confusing, since I
> thought it would mean that it would try to fulfill the alloactions
> from within a packet first, then fall back to remote packets if that
> failed. (Or maybe I am just misunderstanding your explanation. Please
> do let me know if that is the case : -) )
> 
> If what I understand is the case , I think this is the same thing as
> just restricting allocations to be socket-local. I also wonder if
> this idea applies to other mempolicies as well (i.e. unweighted interleave)
> 

I was thinking about this as well, and in my head i think you have to
consider a 2x2 situation

cpuset             |   multi-socket-cpu      single-socket-cpu
==================================================================
single-socket-mem  |     mem-package            mem-package
------------------------------------------------------------------
multi-socket-mem   |       global                 global
------------------------------------------------------------------

But I think this reduces to cpuset nodes dictates the weights used -
which should already be the case with the existing code.

I think you are right that we need to be very explicit about the
fallback semantics here - but that may just be a matter of dictating
whether the allocation falls back or prefers direct reclaim to push
pages out of their requested nodes.

~Gregory

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds per-policy page allocation counters to track allocations driven by each memory policy (mempolicy) on a NUMA node. The new counters, exposed in /proc/vmstat and memory.numa_stat, follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and provide detailed information about intentional vs fallback allocations. The counters are tracked per-memcg (memory control group) and per-node, allowing for more accurate investigation of pressure on a NUMA node.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the stats exposed via /proc/vmstat are guarded by CONFIG_NUMA, not CONFIG_MEMCG, and suggested using mod_node_page_state to make global counters work regardless of cgroup configuration.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello JP!

The stats are exposed via /proc/vmstat and are guarded by CONFIG_NUMA, not
CONFIG_MEMCG. Early returning overhere would make it inaccuate. Does
it make sense to use mod_node_page_state if memcg is not available,
so that these global counters work regardless of cgroup configuration.

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about inconsistent behavior when memcg is disabled, proposed an if-else statement to handle this case and update stats accordingly.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt expressed concern about the number of new metrics added per-memcg, citing a significant memory cost for each additional metric.
review_comments.raw_body:
I have not looked into what these metrics mean but these are too many, at least
for the memcg. For the memcg, there is significant memory cost for each metric
added to memcg.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt noted that the new page allocation counters are monotonic increasing metrics, suggesting they are more useful as a rate of change rather than their absolute value, and asked if tracepoints and BPF could be used to achieve this instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
This seems like monotonic increasing metrics and I think you don't care about
their absolute value but rather rate of change. Any reason this can not be
achieved through tracepoints and BPF combination?

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged Shakeel's concern about the placement of new statistics, explaining they are intended for use alongside existing per-node reclaim stats and would be collected from the same source.
review_comments.raw_body:
We have the per-node reclaim stats (pg{steal,scan,refill}) in
nodeN/vmstat and memory.numa_stat now. The new stats in this patch would
be collected from the same source. They were meant to be used together,
so it seemed like a reasonable location. I think the advantage over
tracepoints is we get the observability on from the start and it would
be simple to extend existing programs that already read stats from the
cgroup dir files.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt expressed concern that adding 18 counters for per-memcg tracking would be too costly, estimating a single counter's cost at 200KiB to MiBs on typical production machines, and suggested decoupling these stats from the rstat infrastructure to reduce overhead.
review_comments.raw_body:
Convenience is not really justifying the cost of adding 18 counters,
particularly in memcg. We can argue about adding just in system level metrics
but not for memcg.

counter_cost = nr_cpus * nr_nodes * nr_memcg * 16 (struct lruvec_stats_percpu)

On a typical prod machine, we can see 1000s of memcg, 100s of cpus and couple of
numa nodes. So, a single counter's cost can range from 200KiB to MiBs. This does
not seem like a cost we should force everyone to pay.

If you really want these per-memcg and assuming these metrics are updated in
non-performance critical path, we can try to decouple these and other reclaim
related stats from rstat infra. That would at least reduce nr_cpus factor in the
above equation to 1. Though we will need to actually evaluate the performance
for the change before committing to it.

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author is willing to compromise on per-cgroup granularity, suggesting a change from tracking page allocations per mempolicy per-memcg to globally per-node statistics.
review_comments.raw_body:
I could trade off the per-cgroup granularity and change these stats to
become global per-node stats.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Johannes Weiner noted that the patch does not handle the !CONFIG_MEMCG case correctly in mem_cgroup_lruvec() and mod_lruvec_state(), and suggested adding a dummy for mem_cgroup_from_task() to simplify the code.
review_comments.raw_body:
mem_cgroup_lruvec() and mod_lruvec_state() already do the right thing
for !CONFIG_MEMCG. Add a dummy for mem_cgroup_from_task() and you can
do a single, shared sequence for both configs.

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Vlastimil expressed concerns that adding more counters (e.g., for preferred vs preferred_many policies) does not provide a clear benefit and may be redundant compared to tracing, requesting alternative solutions.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think I've been on of the folks on previous versions arguing against the
many counters, and one of the arguments was it they can't tell the full
story anyway (compared to e.g. tracing), but I don't think adding even more
counters is the right solution. Seems like a number of other people
responding to the thread are providing similar feedback.

For example I'm still not sure how it would help me if I knew the
hits/misses were due to a preferred vs preferred_many policy, or interleave
vs weithed interleave?

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the number and complexity of policy-specific page allocation counters, proposing to simplify the implementation by using a single aggregated policy triplet (hit/miss/foreign) per policy instead of triplets for each policy.
review_comments.raw_body:
How about I change from per-policy hit/miss/foreign triplets to a single
aggregated policy triplet (i.e. just 3 new counters which account for
all policies)? They would follow the same hit/miss/foreign semantics
already proposed (visible in quoted text above). This would still
provide the otherwise missing signal of whether policy-driven
allocations to a node are intentional or fallback.

Note that I am also planning on moving the stats off of the memcg so the
3 new counters will be global per-node in response to similar feedback.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCHv7.1 17/18] hugetlb: Update vmemmap_dedup.rst
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the documentation for vmemmap optimization in hugetlb to reflect changes in how the kernel maps tail pages. The optimization is applied when the size of struct page is a power of 2, allowing remapping of tail pages to a shared read-only page and freeing original vmemmap pages.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)
review_comments.summary: The patch updates the documentation to reflect changes in how the kernel maps tail pages. The optimization is applied when the size of struct page is a power of 2, allowing remapping of tail pages to a shared read-only page and freeing original vmemmap pages.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v5 05/22] x86/virt/seamldr: Retrieve P-SEAMLDR information
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Patch v5 05/22 aims to retrieve P-SEAMLDR information, but its implementation and caching strategy are being debated.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the patch's performance and suggested caching the response to improve efficiency. However, Chao Gao countered that adding complexity for caching might not be worth it.

review_comments.author: Chao Gao
review_comments.summary: Addressed Kiryl's concerns by explaining that the num_remaining_updates field changes after each update, making caching less beneficial. Also mentioned Dave's suggestion to allocate the structure on the stack.
review_comments.raw_body:
>> +
>> +int seamldr_get_info(struct seamldr_info *seamldr_info)
>> +{
>> +	/*
>> +	 * Use slow_virt_to_phys() since @seamldr_info may be allocated on
>> +	 * the stack.
>> +	 */
>> +	struct tdx_module_args args = { .rcx = slow_virt_to_phys(seamldr_info) };
>> +
>> +	return seamldr_call(P_SEAMLDR_INFO, &args);
>
>On what condition this information can change?

After each update, the num_remaining_updates field will change.

>
>I see the next patch calls this on every _show operation. Would we
>benefit from caching the response?

Yes, caching would save a few cycles, but adds complexity. we'd need to keep
the cache synchronized and invalidate it when values change, likely requiring
additional state tracking.

Dave recommended allocating the structure on the stack [*] when I raised the
caching idea.

This isn't a hot path, so I prefer keeping it "simple and stupid".

[*]: https://lore.kernel.org/all/56a62e54-59f7-4423-9f01-4472d5c3815d@intel.com/

>
>> +}
>> +EXPORT_SYMBOL_FOR_MODULES(seamldr_get_info, "tdx-host");
>> -- 
>> 2.47.3
>> 
>
>-- 
>  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 3/4] x86/virt/tdx: Add SEAMCALL wrapper for TDH.SYS.DISABLE
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds a SEAMCALL wrapper for TDH.SYS.DISABLE, which is used to disable the Trusted Domain Extensions (TDX) hypervisor. The feature is not yet finalized and its support is enumerated by TDX_FEATURES0. The patch addresses a potential issue where the next patch in the series calls this function unconditionally without checking if it's supported.

review_comments.author: Edgecombe, P
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned the necessity of checking TDX_FEATURES0 bit before calling TDH.SYS.DISABLE, citing that this scenario is exotic and future modules will have the feature, potentially making the check redundant.

Reviewer suggested adding a warn for seamcall failures, noting that previous kexec patches had trouble solving races around tdx enabling and recommending handling TDX_SYS_BUSY as well.
review_comments.raw_body:
We debated checking the feature bit before allowing kexec, but decided it was
simpler to just blindly call and ignore the errors. The reasoning was that this
is already a somewhat exotic scenario being addressed, and future modules will
have the feature. So maintaining a check for the feature bit only helps a little
bit, for a short time. And then only if the user would rather have kexec blocked
than attempt it. Do you think it is worth it?

---

Do you think it's worth a warn? There are a couple other considerations.
  - Kai brought up offline that we should handle TDX_SYS_BUSY here too.
  - Previous kexec patches had trouble solving races around tdx enabling. So we
have to handle the seamcall failures.

So we have to exclude a few different errors in different ways. And then the
warn worthy error codes either don't impact anything, or the new kernel will
fail to initialize the TDX module and give notice there.

I don't have a strong objection. It seems to be a judgment call of whether the
complexity is worth the benefit.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] arm64: request contpte-sized folios for exec memory
series_items.patch_summary: The patch modifies the exec_folio_order() function to request contpte-sized folios for executable memory on arm64 architectures. This change aims to optimize page cache allocation by taking into account the hardware's ability to coalesce contiguous PTEs, resulting in fewer iTLB entries.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: No comment provided.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: bypass mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead
series_items.patch_summary: The patch bypasses the mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead by moving the targeted readahead block above the mmap_miss check in do_sync_mmap_readahead(). This change allows arch-preferred folio orders to be requested for executable memory, which can improve performance. The mmap_miss counter is incremented on every page cache miss and decremented when pages are successfully mapped via fault-around or found in cache.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the patch's approach to readahead for executable memory. Suggested that the patch should also consider the case where async_size is not zero, and that the targeted readahead block should be moved above the mmap_miss check in do_sync_mmap_readahead().

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adjusts the alignment of ELF binaries (ET_DYN) to ensure that their load address is aligned to the size of large folios allocated for execution memory. This is necessary because some architectures, such as arm64, require a specific alignment for contpte mapping to work correctly. Without this adjustment, the readahead patches cannot benefit from contpte mapping, leading to reduced iTLB coalescing benefits. The patch introduces a new calculation for ELF alignment based on the arch's requested folio size, ensuring that virtual addresses of exec pages are aligned to the folio boundary.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author is addressing a concern about the alignment of ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping, specifically on ARM architecture where 64K page size is used. They explain that they chose to align with 2M (cont-pte size) to get both page fault benefit and iTLB coverage, which is already the case on x86.

The author is addressing a concern that the patch only checks for alignment when there's at least one PT_LOAD segment large enough to contain a full folio, and proposes to check all segments with PT_LOAD instead. The author agrees that this makes sense and suggests applying the change over the entire series.
review_comments.raw_body:
For us it came about because we use 64K page size on ARM, and none of the
text sections were getting hugified (because PMD size is 512M). I went with
exec_folio_order() = cont-pte size (2M) for 16K and 64K as we can get both page
fault benefit (which might not be that important) and iTLB coverage (due to
cont-pte).
x86 already faults in at 2M (HPAGE_PMD_ORDER) due to force_thp_readahead path in
do_sync_mmap_readahead() so the memory pressure introduced in ARM won't be worse
than what already exists in x86.

---

Yes I think this makes sense!

Although maybe we should check all segments with PT_LOAD. So maybe something
like below over this series?

diff --git a/fs/binfmt_elf.c b/fs/binfmt_elf.c
index 2d2b3e9fd474f..a0e83b541a7d8 100644
--- a/fs/binfmt_elf.c
+++ b/fs/binfmt_elf.c
@@ -1116,10 +1116,30 @@ static int load_elf_binary(struct linux_binprm *bprm)
                         * the hardware cannot coalesce PTEs (e.g. arm64
                         * contpte) even though the physical memory and
                         * file offset are correctly aligned.
+                        *
+                        * Only increase alignment when at least one
+                        * PT_LOAD segment is large enough to contain a
+                        * full folio and has its file offset and virtual
+                        * address folio-aligned. This avoids reducing
+                        * ASLR entropy for small binaries that cannot
+                        * benefit from contpte mapping.
                         */
-                       if (exec_folio_order())
-                               alignment = max(alignment,
-                                       (unsigned long)PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order());
+                       if (exec_folio_order()) {
+                               unsigned long folio_sz = PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order();
+
+                               for (i = 0; i < elf_ex->e_phnum; i++) {
+                                       if (elf_phdata[i].p_type != PT_LOAD)
+                                               continue;
+                                       if (elf_phdata[i].p_filesz < folio_sz)
+                                               continue;
+                                       if (!IS_ALIGNED(elf_phdata[i].p_vaddr, folio_sz))
+                                               continue;
+                                       if (!IS_ALIGNED(elf_phdata[i].p_offset, folio_sz))
+                                               continue;
+                                       alignment = max(alignment, folio_sz);
+                                       break;
+                               }
+                       }
 
                        /**
                         * DOC: PIE handling

review_comments.author: hev

review_comments.raw_body:
I think this logic should live in maximum_alignment(), so we don't
have to walk the segments twice. It might be better to move it into a
separate helper, something like should_align_to_exec_folio()?

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] mm: align file-backed mmap to exec folio order in thp_get_unmapped_area
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses an issue where shared libraries loaded by ld.so via mmap() get 64K-aligned virtual addresses, preventing contpte mapping even when large folios are allocated. The patch introduces a fallback alignment in thp_get_unmapped_area_vmflags() that tries PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order() alignment (2M on arm64 64K pages) when PMD_SIZE alignment fails.

review_comments.author: WANG Rui
review_comments.summary: Identified an issue where even when the code segment satisfies PMD_SIZE, it still doesn't end up at a PMD-aligned virtual address. Suggested skipping the PMD_SIZE == folio_sz case to avoid calling __thp_get_unmapped_area() twice with the same parameters.
review_comments.raw_body:
> +	if (exec_folio_order()) {
> +		unsigned long folio_size = PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order();
> +
> +		ret = __thp_get_unmapped_area(filp, addr, len, off, flags,
> +					      folio_size, vm_flags);
> +		if (ret)
> +			return ret;
> +	}
> +

I noticed that even when the code segment of a user-space shared library
satisfies PMD_SIZE (32MB), it still doesn\u2019t end up at a PMD-aligned virtual
address. This might be the fallback you mentioned. Adjusting p_align in the
ld.so ELF loader does work, though it also avoids extremely large PMD_SIZE
values (capped at \u226432M).

It would probably be better to skip the PMD_SIZE == folio_sz case here,
so we don\u2019t end up calling __thp_get_unmapped_area() twice with the same
parameters.

Thanks,
Rui

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues with contpte-sized exec folios on arm64, specifically for 16K and 64K page configurations. The first issue is that the current implementation returns an incorrect order for these page sizes, which is fixed by using a different calculation. The second issue is that readahead is disabled after 100 page faults due to the mmap_miss heuristic, but this only happens on 64K pages because the decrement paths are inactive. This is fixed by moving the VM_EXEC readahead block above the mmap_miss check. Finally, the patch ensures that the virtual address is aligned to CONT_PTE_SIZE (2M) for contpte mapping, which requires alignment of the VMA start address.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David questioned the assumption that a single order is sufficient for contpte mapping, suggesting instead to make decisions based on the mapping size.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm curious: is a single order really what we want?

I'd instead assume that we might want to make decisions based on the
mapping size.

Assume you have a 128M mapping, wouldn't we want to use a different
alignment than, say, for a 1M mapping, a 128K mapping or a 8k mapping?

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.summary:
Reviewer WANG Rui noted that rustc's performance regressed after a binutils upgrade and is working on making ld.so aware of THP, which would adjust PT_LOAD alignment to increase the chances of shared libraries being mapped by THP. He mentioned doing similar work in the kernel for executables.

Reviewer WANG Rui suggested adding constraints to increase p_align only when the segment length, virtual address, and file offset are all huge-aligned, citing a reduction in ASLR entropy as motivation.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Usama,

Glad to see you're pushing on this, I'm also following it. I first noticed this when rustc's perf regressed after a binutils upgrade. I'm trying to make ld.so to aware THP and adjust PT_LOAD alignment to increase the chances of shared libraries being mapped by THP [1]. As you're probably seen, I'm doing something similar in the kernel to improve it for executables [2].

I\u2019m curious, does it make sense to add some constraints here, like only increasing p_align when the segment length, virtual address, and file offset are all huge-aligned, as I did in my patch? review_comments.author: Ryan Roberts
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Ryan Roberts expressed concern about potential read amplification due to large default alignment of 2M, which may lead to unnecessary memory reads on systems with smaller page sizes like Android's 16K config.

Reviewer Ryan Roberts questioned whether it is feasible to influence ELF file alignment from the kernel, pointing out that the text section may not be the first section in the file and its alignment depends on the dynamic loader.

Reviewer Ryan Roberts suggested aligning the text section to 2M and using MADV_HUGEPAGE to enable contpte mapping, instead of relying on readahead.
review_comments.raw_body:
This was deliberate, although perhaps a bit conservative. I was concerned about
the possibility of read amplification; pointlessly reading in a load of memory
that never actually gets used. And that is independent of page size.

2M seems quite big as a default IMHO, I could imagine Android might complain
about memory pressure in their 16K config, for example.

Additionally, ELF files are normally only aligned to 64K and you can only get
the TLB benefits if the memory is aligned in physical and virtual memory.

---

I don't see how you can reliably influence this from the kernel? The ELF file
alignment is, by default, 64K (16K on Android) and there is no guarrantee that
the text section is the first section in the file. You need to align the start
of the text section to the 2M boundary and to do that, you'll need to align the
start of the file to some 64K boundary at a specific offset to the 2M boundary,
based on the size of any sections before the text section. That's a job for the
dynamic loader I think? Perhaps I've misunderstood what you're doing...

---

I think the proper way to do this is to link the text section with 2M alignment
and have the dynamic linker mark the region with MADV_HUGEPAGE?

Thanks,
Ryan

review_comments.raw_body:

faults are not that big of a deal? If the text section is hot, it wont
get flushed after faulting in. So the real benefit comes from improved

The author addressed concerns about memory pressure due to contpte mapping on 16K and 64K pages by comparing it to x86's behavior and explaining that the readahead is clamped to VMA boundaries, reducing potential memory pressure. The author does not agree with the reviewer's implication that the current code would lead to worse memory pressure.

The author acknowledges that the virtual address of shared libraries may not be aligned to the contpte size due to the get_unmapped_area callback trying PMD_SIZE alignment first, and explains how patch 4 addresses this issue by adding a fallback for folio-size alignment.

Author acknowledged that the force_thp_readahead path triggered by MADV_HUGEPAGE reads at HPAGE_PMD_ORDER (512M) on arm64 with 64K pages, and suggested using a dynamic linker as an alternate approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
The force_thp_readahead path in do_sync_mmap_readahead() reads at
HPAGE_PMD_ORDER (2M on x86) and even doubles it to 4M for
non VM_RAND_READ mappings (ra->size *= 2), with async readahead
enabled. exec_folio_order() is more conservative. a single 2M folio
with async_size=0, no speculative prefetch. So I think the memory
pressure would not be worse than what x86 has?

For memory pressure on Android 16K: the readahead is clamped to VMA
boundaries, so a small shared library won't read 2M.
page_cache_ra_order() reduces folio order near EOF and on allocation
failure, so the 2M order is a preference, not a guarantee with the
current code?

---

I only started looking into how this works a few days before sending these
patches, so I could be wrong (please do correct me if thats the case!)

For the main binary (patch 3): load_elf_binary() controls load_bias.
Each PT_LOAD segment is mapped at load_bias + p_vaddr via elf_map().
The alignment variable feeds directly into load_bias calculation.
If p_vaddr=0 and p_offset=0, mapped_addr = load_bias + 0 = load_bias. By
ensuring load_bias is folio size aligned, the text segment's virtual address
is also folio size aligned.

For shared libraries (patch 4): ld.so loads these via mmap(), and the
kernel's get_unmapped_area callback (thp_get_unmapped_area for ext4,
xfs, btrfs) picks the virtual address. The existing code tries
PMD_SIZE alignment first (512M on 64K pages), which is too large for
typical shared libraries and always fails. Patch 4 adds a fallback
that tries folio-size alignment (2M), which is small enough to succeed
for most libraries.

---

On arm64 with 64K pages, the force_thp_readahead path triggered by
MADV_HUGEPAGE reads at HPAGE_PMD_ORDER (512M). Even with file and
anon khugepaged support aded for khugpaged, the collapse won't happen
form the start.

Yes I think dynamic linker is also a good alternate approach from Wangs
patches [1]. But doing it in the kernel would be more transparent?

[1] https://sourceware.org/pipermail/libc-alpha/2026-March/175776.html

review_comments.author: WANG Rui
review_comments.summary: Reviewer WANG Rui noted that most AArch64 and LoongArch64 binaries place the executable segment in the first PT_LOAD, satisfying alignment requirements for PMD-sized or large folio mappings, but this is not true for x86 due to linker defaults, specifically -z separate-code being the default on x86.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Arch Linux for AArch64 and LoongArch64 I've observed that most
binaries place the executable segment in the first PT_LOAD. In that
case both the virtual address and file offset are 0, which happens to
satisfy the alignment requirements for PMD-sized or large folio
mappings.

x86 looks quite different. The executable segment is usually not the
first one.

After digging into this I realized this mostly comes from the linker
defaults. With GNU ld, -z noseparate-code merges the read-only and
read-only-executable segments into one, while -z separate-code
splits them, placing a non-executable read-only segment first. The
latter is the default on x86, partly to avoid making the ELF headers
executable when mappings start from the beginning of the file.

Other architectures tend to default to -z noseparate-code, which
makes it more likely that the text segment is the first PT_LOAD.

LLVM lld behaves differently again: --rosegment (equivalent to
-z separate-code) is enabled by default on all architectures, which
similarly tends to place the executable segment after an initial
read-only one. That default is clearly less friendly to large-page
text mappings.

Thanks,
Rui
